caveats: a: The occupation includes guards employed directly by customers and workers in adjacent investigation and armored-car activities.
a: No source measures the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market contract-guard firms.
a: Exposure varies sharply between static camera-monitoring posts, mobile patrols, armed protection, access control, and event security.
a: The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, creating a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.

rho: SIA is a security-technology industry association and describes capabilities and safeguards rather than representative realized savings.
rho: Customer ownership of cameras, access systems, and networks can slow contractor-led implementation.
rho: GAO's federal contract-guard findings are a public-sector case and may not generalize to commercial sites.
e: The Census definition does not measure recurring revenue, contract transferability, or the eligible share of LMM firms.
e: Special-event and bodyguard work may be repeat business but can be materially less recurring than staffed-site contracts.
e: The injected count of 625 uses a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge that may not reflect thin-margin, wage-intensive guard firms.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
s5: No industry-specific owner-age or completed-control-deal series was found.
s5: Customer change-of-control rights, licensing, insurance, wage claims, and contract concentration can prevent a planned sale from qualifying.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for US lower-middle-market guard contractors.
q: Retention differs between fixed-price outcomes, per-post contracts, and cost-plus or per-hour staffing.
q: Customer security budgets may convert labor savings into expanded coverage rather than provider margin.
d5: BLS forecasts occupation employment across outsourced and captive employers, not purchased 561612 service quantity.
d5: GAO examines one federal program and does not represent commercial contract demand.
d5: Guard demand varies with office occupancy, retail conditions, events, crime concerns, public budgets, and customer technology adoption.
o: Occupational duties do not measure the fraction of contracted service quantity that customers will continue purchasing.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial property owners, residential communities, healthcare and educational institutions, industrial sites, retailers, event operators, government agencies, and other organizations buy guard hours, staffed posts, patrol routes, screening, or protection under recurring contracts, repeat work orders, or scheduled events, commonly priced per labor hour, post, route, or fixed contract period.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house guards, public police, installation or remote monitoring of electronic security systems classified in NAICS 56162, armored-car services, investigation services, one-off engagements without a repeat service relationship, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced staffed security posts, mobile patrols, access control, visitor screening, parking security, event security, bodyguard, guard-dog, alarm-response, and related on-site protective services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe the security-guard occupation across outsourced and captive employers rather than a wage-weighted task study of 561612 firms. I map camera and alarm observation, routine access-log review, dispatch assistance, scheduling, patrol documentation, incident-report drafting, and administrative communication to remaining AI exposure, while leaving visible deterrence, physical patrol, identity and bag screening, confrontation, escort, first aid, emergency response, and site-specific judgment largely unexposed. Because 59% of guards are in investigation, guard, and armored-car services, the occupation is directionally close; inclusion of captive guards may bias the mapping either way, and already installed video and access-control systems may overstate not-yet-automated opportunity.
caveats: The occupation includes guards employed directly by customers and workers in adjacent investigation and armored-car activities.
No source measures the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market contract-guard firms.
Exposure varies sharply between static camera-monitoring posts, mobile patrols, armed protection, access control, and event security.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, creating a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across contract-guard firms that separates already automated monitoring from patrol, access control, screening, incident response, supervision, scheduling, dispatch, reporting, and administration would materially change this value.
a.rationale: AI video analytics, alarm triage, scheduling, dispatch support, and report generation can reduce monitoring and administrative hours or allow each supervisor and remote operator to cover more sites. Most paid guard time still purchases physical presence, deterrence, patrol, access intervention, emergency response, and accountable action, keeping the exposed share below one-third at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects essentially flat security-guard employment from 2024 to 2034, says guards remain needed because of crime and vandalism concerns, and says AI-enabled remote monitoring may limit employment. GAO documents continuing reliance on 13,000 contract guards at federal facilities and operational harm from shortages. I translate these occupation and federal-site indicators into roughly stable five-year constant-price demand for the current outsourced service basket, allowing modest growth from safety concerns and outsourcing and downside from technology and customer budget pressure. Employment can understate service quantity when productivity rises and the federal case may overstate public-sector persistence, so the net direction is uncertain.
caveats: BLS forecasts occupation employment across outsourced and captive employers, not purchased 561612 service quantity.
GAO examines one federal program and does not represent commercial contract demand.
Guard demand varies with office occupancy, retail conditions, events, crime concerns, public budgets, and customer technology adoption.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price US contract-guard revenue, staffed-post counts, billable hours, patrol stops, customer security budgets, contract awards, and insourcing or technology-substitution losses, segmented by end market, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Organizations continue to need protection, access control, screening, patrol, and emergency response across occupied facilities. Demand is mature rather than rapidly growing, and remote monitoring can reduce staffed hours at low-risk posts, but replacement needs, guard shortages, and the physical-response requirement support a stable core.

caveats: The Census definition does not measure recurring revenue, contract transferability, or the eligible share of LMM firms.
Special-event and bodyguard work may be repeat business but can be materially less recurring than staffed-site contracts.
The injected count of 625 uses a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge that may not reflect thin-margin, wage-intensive guard firms.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 561612 roster matched to tax records and coded for recurring revenue, external-customer mix, contract duration and assignment, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, licensing, insurance, and customer concentration would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition is already centered on outsourced guard and patrol services, and recurring staffed posts, patrol routes, access control, and scheduled-event relationships fit the default lens. Ineligibility remains for predominantly one-off assignments, firms dependent on a nontransferable principal, captive units misclassified into the code, weakly documented cash businesses, or operations without transferable customer contracts.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561612 ($31,693,782,072) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561612 ($37,274,030,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $48,745 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561612); class <5: 2,797 firms, avg revenue/firm $211,033 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $33,027 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 967 firms, avg revenue/firm $416,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,129 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,121 firms, avg revenue/firm $770,186 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $120,534 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,026 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,197,557 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $343,918 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 625 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,686,392 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,515,920 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 171 firms, avg revenue/firm $145,682,561 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,799,321 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 625

o.bridge: BLS describes physical patrol, access control, deterrence, screening, emergency response, and reporting while explicitly stating that AI-enabled remote monitoring may limit some guard employment. O*NET shows high importance for locking entrances, patrol, alarm response, circulation among people, access authorization, first aid, and emergency calls, and GAO shows federal facilities closing when contract guards were unavailable. I translate those tasks and operating evidence into the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable guard or patrol operator. Public and federal sites may overstate physical-presence requirements, while high-liability commercial sites may similarly require response and accountability.
caveats: Occupational duties do not measure the fraction of contracted service quantity that customers will continue purchasing.
Remote monitoring and automated access control can fully replace some low-risk static posts.
Federal facility evidence may overstate operator requirements relative to commercial sites, while armed and high-risk assignments may require more.
o.change_evidence: Customer post orders, insurer and regulator requirements, incident data, contract renewals, and site-level before-and-after studies showing which staffed posts can be removed or self-served without degrading response, deterrence, or liability coverage would materially change this share.
o.rationale: AI can watch more feeds, identify anomalies, automate routine entry, and improve dispatch, but cameras and software cannot generally confront intruders, inspect bags, escort people, administer first aid, manage crowds, secure a failed door, or take responsibility at an incident. Low-risk static observation posts and routine access points face the most elimination or customer self-service.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for US lower-middle-market guard contractors.
Retention differs between fixed-price outcomes, per-post contracts, and cost-plus or per-hour staffing.
Customer security budgets may convert labor savings into expanded coverage rather than provider margin.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and bid dataset showing pricing unit, wage-escalation and technology clauses, renewal cadence, customer concentration, rebid outcomes, guard-hours by post, overtime, and gross-margin changes after AI or remote-monitoring deployment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed-price and per-post contracts can retain savings from lower monitoring, scheduling, supervision, overtime, and reporting effort until renewal. Retention is limited because many contracts are transparently priced per guard-hour, wage and benefit costs are passed through, public and enterprise customers rebid frequently, competitors can buy similar technology, and customers may demand that savings fund broader coverage or lower rates. Technology, integration, training, insurance, cyber, and privacy costs absorb additional benefit.

rho.bridge: BLS says AI-integrated remote monitoring may limit some guard employment, and SIA describes AI video systems that recognize people, vehicles, objects, and events and generate alarms while reducing surveillance fatigue. I translate demonstrated technical availability into five-year implementation of the exposed guard workflow, then haircut for installed-camera coverage, integration with customer systems, false alarms, privacy and cyber controls, labor agreements, insurance, customer acceptance, capital, and the need for human response. Technology-industry guidance likely overstates smooth deployment at smaller guard companies, while standardized monitoring and reporting may diffuse faster than assumed.
caveats: BLS provides an occupational outlook, not a measured adoption rate for 561612 firms.
SIA is a security-technology industry association and describes capabilities and safeguards rather than representative realized savings.
Customer ownership of cameras, access systems, and networks can slow contractor-led implementation.
GAO's federal contract-guard findings are a public-sector case and may not generalize to commercial sites.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent contract-guard firms reporting site-level AI and remote-monitoring deployment, customer approvals, capital and integration cost, false-alarm rates, guard-hours displaced or avoided, response outcomes, and contract renewal effects would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Video analytics, digital post orders, workforce scheduling, mobile patrol verification, automated logs, and report drafting are operational technologies rather than distant concepts. Implementation remains uneven because guard contractors often operate on customer-owned sites and systems, cannot control camera quality or network access, and must prove reliability in safety-critical, privacy-sensitive settings.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control acquisitions of security-guard firms. I haircut the reported 22% intention rate for plans that do not close, family or internal transfers, license and insurance requirements, customer consent, contract rebids, labor liabilities, and owner dependence. The broad survey may overstate qualifying external transfers, though guard businesses with management depth and recurring site contracts may be more transferable than personal-service firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
No industry-specific owner-age or completed-control-deal series was found.
Customer change-of-control rights, licensing, insurance, wage claims, and contract concentration can prevent a planned sale from qualifying.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, security-business brokers, state licensing records, and customer award notices, matched to the eligible firm roster and distinguishing external control deals from internal transfers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A large employer base and recurring customer contracts create plausible transaction supply, but qualifying control transfers must preserve licenses, bonding, insurance, supervisors, recruiting capability, and customer relationships. Planned transfers materially exceed completed external acquisitions.

principal_driver: AI-assisted monitoring, scheduling, dispatch, and reporting can improve coverage per labor hour across a very wage-intensive recurring service.
principal_weakness: Most guard value is embodied physical presence sold by the hour, limiting both substitutable task share and the provider's ability to retain savings.
